perturbation.original_text: 11. This Agreement shall terminate three (3) years after the Effective Date and shall be binding upon the Recipient's heirs, successors and assigns.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. This Agreement shall terminate one (1) year after the Effective Date and shall be binding upon the Recipient's heirs, successors and assigns.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination period of the agreement to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, conflicting with common law standards for trade secret protection, especially since the contract is governed by California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's CUTSA protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide economic value. A one-year term might be inadequate to safeguard certain trade secrets, potentially undermining the protections intended by CUTSA. The reasonableness of the duration of confidentiality is fact-dependent but the contract should not unduly restrict the use of information which the law deems protectable for a longer period.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the agreement termination period from three years to one year. According to the Law Explanation, this reduction might be inadequate to safeguard certain trade secrets, potentially undermining the protections intended by CUTSA. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where time-sensitive obligations (termination period) do not align with legal requirements (trade secret protection). Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), suggesting the agreement's altered term might not adequately protect trade secrets as intended under California law. The one-year term introduces an inconsistency, potentially violating CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: 18. All notices or reports permitted or required under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be delivered by personal delivery, electronic mail, facsimile transmission or by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, and shall be deemed given upon personal delivery, five (5) days after deposit in the mail, or upon acknowledgment of receipt of electronic transmission.
perturbation.changed_text: 18. All notices or reports permitted or required under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be delivered by personal delivery, electronic mail, facsimile transmission or by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, and shall be deemed given upon personal delivery, ten (10) days after deposit in the mail, or upon acknowledgment of receipt of electronic transmission.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the notice period after mailing to ten days conflicts with California's Code of Civil Procedure which specifies shorter timeframes for certain legal notices.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 generally adds two calendar days to the prescribed period when service is by mail within California and longer if outside California, but not ten days. Thus, a contractual requirement of ten days may be unenforceable if it conflicts with statutory deadlines for responses or actions triggered by such notice, potentially prejudicing a party's legal rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of Californi
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the notice period after mailing to ten days, while California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013(a) specifies that the period of notice should be extended by five calendar days if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, and 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California. The original text had five days, which is closer to the law. The new text, requiring ten days regardless of location, directly conflicts with the statute's provision for a five-day extension within California, thus creating a legal inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: 13. Each party acknowledges that its breach of the Agreement will cause irreparable damage and hereby agrees that the other party shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief under this Agreement, as well as such further relief as may be granted by a court of competent jurisdiction. Additionally, in the event of a breach by the Recipient, the disclosing party shall be entitled to recover the costs of enforcing this Agreement including, without limitation reasonable attorneys' fees.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Each party acknowledges that its breach of the Agreement will cause irreparable damage and hereby agrees that the other party shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief under this Agreement, as well as such further relief as may be granted by a court of competent jurisdiction. Additionally, in the event of a breach by the Recipient, the disclosing party shall be entitled to recover the costs of enforcing this Agreement excluding attorneys' fees.
perturbation.explanation: Preventing the disclosing party from recovering attorney's fees in a breach of contract action, despite a clause indicating irreparable harm, could be seen as conflicting with California Civil Code provisions that allow for attorney's fees in contract disputes, especially when the contract involves trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1717 provides for reciprocal attorney's fees in contract actions where the contract provides for attorney's fees. While parties can waive this right, a complete exclusion of attorney's fees for the disclosing party, especially in a scenario involving irreparable harm and potential misappropriation of trade secrets, is unusual and disfavored, potentially raising enforceability issues.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1717 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1717. (a) In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs. Where a contract provides for attorney’s fees, as set forth above, that provision shall be construed as applying to the entire contract, unless each party was represented by counsel in the negotiation and execution of the contract, and the fact of that representation is specified in the contract. Reasonable attorney’s fees shall be fixed by the court, and shall be an element of the costs of suit. Attorney’s fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract which is entered into after the effective date of this section. Any provision in any such contract which provides for a waiver of attorney’s f
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the disclosing party's right to recover attorney's fees in a breach of contract action. The original text stated the disclosing party "shall be entitled to recover the costs of enforcing this Agreement including, without limitation reasonable attorneys' fees." The changed text removes "reasonable attorneys' fees". California Civil Code § 1717, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that in any action on a contract where the contract provides that attorney's fees shall be awarded to enforce the contract, the prevailing party shall be entitled to reasonable attorney's fees. While it's possible to waive this right under specific conditions (representation by counsel specified in the contract), the snippet states that "Attorney’s fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract which is entered into after the effective date of this section." This removal directly contradicts the provision for attorney's fees, creating a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both indicate that this change is unusual and potentially raises enforceability issues, especially in cases involving irreparable harm and trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Upon termination or expiration of the Agreement, or upon written request of the other party, each party shall promptly destroy or return to the other all documents and other tangible materials representing the other's Confidential Information and all copies thereof. The Recipient agrees to destroy all documents, memoranda, notes and other writings whatsoever prepared by the Recipient or its employees or representatives based on the information contained in the Confidential Information (except for references or summaries appearing in minutes or corporate records).
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Upon termination or expiration of the Agreement, or upon written request of the other party, each party shall promptly destroy or return to the other all documents and other tangible materials representing the other's Confidential Information and all copies thereof. The Recipient agrees to destroy all documents, memoranda, notes and other writings whatsoever prepared by the Recipient or its employees or representatives based on the information contained in the Confidential Information (including references or summaries appearing in minutes or corporate records).
perturbation.explanation: Requiring the destruction of references or summaries appearing in minutes or corporate records may violate corporate record-keeping requirements under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code § 1500 requires corporations to keep adequate and correct books and records of account, minutes of proceedings of its shareholders, directors, and committees of the board. Mandating the destruction of summaries appearing in minutes may lead to a violation of these corporate record-keeping requirements. Deleting board minutes risks fines and other punishments.

CORPORATIONS [100 - 14707] ( Title 1 enacted by Stats. 1947, Ch. 1038. ) DIVISION 1. GENERAL CORPORATION LAW [100 - 2319] ( Division 1 repealed and added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) CHAPTER 15. Records and Reports [1500 - 1512] ( Chapter 15 added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) 1500. Each corporation shall keep adequate and correct books and records of account and shall keep minutes of the proceedings of its shareholders, board and committees of the board and shall keep at its principal office, or at the office of its transfer agent or registrar, a record of its shareholders, giving the names and addresses of all shareholders and the number and class of shares held by each. Those minutes and other books and records shall be kept either in written form or in another form capable of being converted into clearly legible tangible form or in any combination of the foregoing. When minutes and other books and records are kept in a form capable of being converted into clearly legible paper form, the clearly legible paper form into which those minutes and other books and records are converted shall be admissible in evidence, and accepted for all other purposes, to the same extent as an original paper record of the same information would have been, provided th
perturbation.justification: The modified text requires the destruction of "references or summaries appearing in minutes or corporate records," while California Corporations Code § 1500, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, mandates that corporations "shall keep minutes of the proceedings of its shareholders, board and committees of the board." Destroying these summaries directly contradicts the legal obligation to maintain corporate minutes, therefore a legal contradiction exists.
